text:
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
1
THE INFLUENCE OF ISLAM ON AN AFRICAN PEOPLE: THE CASE OF THE DAGOMBA OF NORTHERN
GHANA.
BY
MUSTAPHA ABDUL-HAMID
LECTURER
DEPARTMENT OF RELIGION AND HUMAN VALUES
UNIVERSITY OF CAPE COAST
CAPE COAST
GHANA. WEST AFRICA.
TELEPHONE: +233204252522/+233266150743
E-MAIL: bawreh@gmail.com
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
2
Introduction
The Dagomba belong to the Mole-Dagbani ethnic group of people. The Mole-Dagbani ethnic group
comprises the Moshi, Mamprusi, Nanumba and Dagomba. They are believed to have descended from
Na Gbewa. Na Gbewa’s progenitor was the legendary Toha-Zie or Red-Hunter believed to have migrated
from Tunga (supposedly east of Lake Chad) to Zamfara in northern Nigeria, through Mali and then to
Gurma in modern day Burkina Faso.1

Toha-Zie as his name suggests is reputed to have been a great warrior who fought in war with the
Malian king. Pleased with his war prowess, the Malian king is said to have given one of her daughters
called Paga Wabga in marriage to Toha-Zie . This marriage begot Kpogonumbo, who remained in Malian
service for sometime, but later migrated southwards to the country of Gurma in present day Burkina
Faso. The ruler of Gurma at that time was Daramani who also gave one his daughters called Suhuyini to
Kpogunumbo and they begot Na Gbewa from whom all the Mole-Dagbani ethnic groups claim descent.2
Traditional Dagomba people like most traditional African ethnic groups, have a three-tier system of
worship: God, the gods and the ancestors. God in Dagbon is called Nawuni. Na in Dagbani means chief
and Wuni means God. Nawuni which refers to the supreme being and creator of the universe is chief
God which means he is at the head of all the gods.

1
 Ivor Wilks, “The Mossi & the Akan States, 1400-1800” in J.F Ajayi & Michael Crowder (eds). History of West Africa
(London: Longman Group, 1985) 470
2
 Wilks, 470
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
3
Thus the Dagomba worship Nawuni through his intermediaries which are the gods and ancestors. Every
Dagomba village had an abode for the gods which is called bugli which really is a sacred grove. Worship
to Nawuni can therefore only be carried out at the sacred grove. The priest who leads the worship
process on behalf of the community is called the tindana which literally translated, means owner of the
earth. Thus the tindana takes care of the land on behalf of the gods and God. The ancestors are called
tiyaan-nima. Every family has ancestors to whom they pay homage and to whom they ask for
intercession with the gods and God. Most of the time sacrifice to the ancestors is made by the explicit
commandment of a soothsayer who in Dagbon is called baga. This happens when a calamity of one form
or another befalls the family such as sickness or death. The baga may then recommand that sacrifice be
made to the ancestors in order that they may intercede with the gods on behalf of the family.
This is the worldview of traditional Dagomba society and for the small percentage of Dagomba people
who remain faithful to the traditional religion, this is their way of communion with God. Less than 10
percent of the Dagomba population still refer to themselves as traditional believers. Even for those who
refer to themselves as traditional believers, they also participate in Islamic rituals of worship and would
occassionally even perform the salat. That is the extent to which Islam has influenced Dagomba life and
thought.
This paper exposes the depths to which Islam has influenced the eidos (lifeways) and ethos
(thoughtways) of the Dagomba people. The first part of the paper, looks at selected Dagomba cultural
norms and practices before the introduction of Islam. This part of the paper was conducted mostly
through interviews with Dagomba elders. The author was also born and raised in Dagbon and still lives
there and therefore some of the material is also borne out of the personal experience of the author. The 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
4
second part of the paper, which discusses the present nature of Dagbon culture after its encounter with
Islam, is largely from the author’s experience and from participant observation. The author has been a
participant in the festivals of Dagbon for well over 30 years now.
Theoretical Framework
This paper has been written from the perspective of acculturation theories. The term acculturation, was
first coined by Redfield, Linton and Herskovits. They defined the term as “phenomena which results
when groups of individuals, having different cultures come into continuous first hand contact with
subsequent changes in the original culture patterns of either or both groups.”3
 Acculturation therefore
refers to the sociocultural changes that take place in the culture of a people, when they have come into
contact with another culture and maintained that contact for a long time.
In 1954, the Social Science Research Council (SSRC) revised the concept of acculturation, adding new
perspectives to the original coinage by Redfield and his associates. The SSRC defined acculturation as
culture change that is initiated by the conjunction of two or more autonomous
culture systems. Its dynamics can be seen as the selective adaptation of value
systems, the processes of integration and differentiation, the generation of

3
 R. Redfield, R. Linton & M. Herskovits, “Memorandum for the Study of Acculturation” in American Anthropologist,
38, 149-152 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
5
developmental sequences and the operation of role determinants and
personality factors4
Thus Spindler and Spindler envisage acculturation as the process whereby a cultural group progresses
from a native or traditional oriented state through a transitional stage to an elite acculturated stage.5
Thus today, there is vitually no traditional Dagomba rite anymore. Dagomba traditions and culture show
heavy islamic influences. From birth through marriage to funeral ceremonies, the influence of Islam on
Dagomaba life and thought is glaringly apparent. This is what this paper now explores.
Dagomba Life And Thought Before The Advent of Islam
Rites of Passage
Birth Rites
Traditional Dagomba perform elaborate religious rites on the occassion of a woman’s first pregnancy.
This stage is considered an anxious moment in the life of the woman because it marks a transformation
or mode of existence from one stage to the other. That is why this stage is marked by religious rites. This
rite is called pirigibu which literally means undressing. This ceremony is the official declaration of a
pregnancy. Before the performance of this ceremony no one is permitted to refer to the pregnancy of a
woman.

4
 Social Science Research Council, “Acculturation & Exploratory Formulation” in American Anthropologist, 56, 974-
1002
5
 L. Spindler & G. Spindler, “Male & Female Adaptations in Culture Change: Menomini” in R. Hunt (ed) Personalities
& Cultures (New York: Natural History Press, 1967) 56-78
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
6
When a woman is pregnant for about three to four months, her husband informs his in-laws of their
daughter’s conception and a date is fixed for the ceremony. A sister of the husband is normally invited
to officiate in the ceremony. Where there is no sister, any female relative of the husband is eligible to
perform the ceremony. She comes to the house with a calabash in her hand and opens the ceremony in
the pregnant woman’s room by giving her a knock on the head or shoulder saying “you were a child, but
now you are an adult.” This knock is supposed to transform her from a girl into a brave woman ready to
bear all the impending pains of puerperium (the period between childbirth and the return of the womb
to its normal state). The cloth that is tied to her waist is then untied and she stands naked with the sign
of pregnancy visible as a result of the protrusion of the belly. A special meal is then prepared with
chicken presented by the officiating woman or under her supervision. The ancestral spirits are then
invoked to protect the woman through out her labouring period and also to ensure a smooth delivery.
To round off the ceremony, a soothsayer is contacted to determine the ancestor after whom the child
should be named when it is born and usually a fowl is slaughtered in memory of such an ancestor. From
then on, the woman can be referred to as pagapulana or pregnant woman.
At child birth there is always a traditional birth attendant, usually an old lady who is experienced in
assisting young women to accomplish a safe delivery. The unbilical cord is cut and buried within the
house or just outside it. The disposal of this cord shows that the child has died from the world of
pregnancy and is alive in the new state of birth. Meanwhile the safe delivery of the child is heralded by
loud ululation by the women present during the process. When it is a baby boy, the women ululate
thrice and if it is girl, four times. It appears that there is no special reason for assigning the numbers 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
7
three to boys and four to girls. It has been an old practice which the Dagomba have not paused to find
its significance.
An old lady is ususally assigned the responsibility of bathing the child twice a day preferably with warm
water. Males are usually called Sandow and females Sanpaga until the eighth day when the naming
ceremony is held and the child is given a name. On the eighth day, the child is named. A sheep is
slaughtered and there is general merry making and feasting. The child’s hair is shaved.6
Marriage
Marriage as an institution has both social and religious dimensions. These are especially emphasised in
Dagbon society. Marriage is not considered as just two people coming together as man and wife . On the
contrary, it is a union between two families rather than individuals, and more so an occassion for the
coming together of the departed, the living and the yet to be born. Therefore to a large extent marriage
is regarded as an important state of the life of man.
Any young man or woman who attains the age of maturity (twenty years for men and sixteen years for
the women) and who does not marry is looked upon with some sort of contempt. By refusing to get
married, the individual is thwarting the growth of the society and by so doing prevent the incarnation of
the ancestor. Since ancestral veneration forms the dominant theme of the traditional religion of the
Dagomba, it will be considered an insult to the ancestors for one to completely stay away from
marriage. For example, some departed great grandfather or grandmother might wish to return into the

6
 This information was gathered and corroborated by six different elderly women, aged between 65-75 in different
locations in Dagbon, namely, Savelugu, Tamale and Nanton. See also Christine Oppong, Growing Up in Dagbon
(Accra: Ghana Publishing Corporation, 1973) 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
8
world through a particular marriage but if that marriage is not made possible, this ancestor cannot
return and so his or her displeasure is incurred.
According to a popular belief, the ancestor can cause havoc not only to this individual but to his family
and clan as well. That is why the institution of marriage in Dagbon is held high and that is also why
parents do not hesitate to bethroth their female children to men.
Marriage is what confers maturity on the Dagomba. It is the duty of parents to find spouses for their
chidren. Female chidren are bethrothed at birth. Parents who are interested in asking for the child’s
hand for their son, send gifts to the mother. From then on, the prospective in-laws will continue to send
foodstuffs and firewood and farm for their prospective in-laws.
When the girl has attained marriageable age, the boy arranges one night to “kidnap” her and take her to
his home. Thus when one night the girl is not found, her parents understand that this may be the
reason. The next day the boy’s parents send gifts of money and cola nut called sandaani or apology to
the girl’s parents. It must however be noted that before marriage arrangements begin, both parents
consult soothsayers to find out whether such a marriage will be a successful one or not and whether
there will be benefits in the form of children.
When the young man is ready to get married, he sends a pot of pito, an empty gourd and a fowl to the
girl’s parents. This ceremony is a clear indicator that the woman will leave the family soon. Then the
young man buys a mukuru which is a kind of skirt made from smock material which the woman wears
when she is taken to the man’s home. 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
9
Polygyny is the norm in Dagbon society. A man with one wife is considered a bachelor because after his
wife delivers, she is required to go and stay with her parents for a period spanning over one year.7
Christine Oppong puts the number of years that a nursing mother stays at her parents house at two
years.8
 Thus for the period that one’s wife is away, one virtually reverts to bachelorhood and that is why
Dagombas consider a person with one wife to be as good as a bachelor. More importantly, the more
children one has, the wealthier one believed to be.
Death
Even though Dagombas accept death as a fact of life, yet they must consult a soothsayer to find out the
cause of a person’s death. People are then sent to relatives far and near to inform them of the death of
a relation. When they announce the death of a person they say that ‘we have been sent to come and tell
you that so-so-and-so is absent.” Women and men sit differently in the funeral home.
The head of the family known as dogrikpema with the help of other relations does the bathing. Men
bathe men and women bathe women. In Dagbon, there are special people responsible for burying the
dead called kasigriba. Before the introduction of weaving, teak leaves were used in burying the dead. It
was called kornamgbandi.
9
 There is another public assembly on the third day after the death of the
person. This day is called boali logbu. Boali logbu ends the period of mourning.
Festivals

7
 Mma Sanatu, aged 70, interview by author, tape recording, Yendi, February 3, 1999.
8
 Oppong, Growing Up, 37
9
 Afa Shero, aged 56, interview by author, tape recording, Kumbungu, June 5 1999. 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
10
Bugum
The bugum festival was celebrated in Dagbon before the advent of Islam. Bugum means fire. The bugum
festival is otherwise known as the fire festival. It is celebrated at the beginning of every lunar year. The
first month of the year for Dagombas is called Bugum.
This festival is celebrated to commemorate the finding of a Dagomba king’s son who went missing for
several days. The search party that found the king’s son, found him sitting under a tree. They therefore
torched the tree for habouring the king’s son. During the bugum festival, all idols, shrines and gods are
cleansed. During this festival, the Dagomba make new resolutions and appeal to the ancestors to guard
and guide them through out the new year. They cut bits and pieces of cooked food and place them on
their gooni (walls). These pieces of food belong to the ancestors and they are supposed to come round
and eat them.
Damba
The Damba festival is celebrated in the third month of the lunar calendar. It is celebrated as a festival of
thanks giving for asking the ancestors and the gods for a prosperous new year. There are two types of
Damba. The first which starts on the tenth day of the month is known as Somo Damba. The second
which starts on the seventeenth of the same month is known as Na Damba. The Somo Damba involves
drumming and dancing at nights through out the week but chiefs do not participate fully in it. The Na
Damba on the other hand is characterised by great pageantry in which colour and melody are displayed
at their best, and it is during this celebration that chiefs take an active part.
Islam in Dagbon 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
11
Islam was introduced into Dagbon in the eighteenth century with the conversion of the Dagomba king,
Na Zangina. Hitherto, Islam had been in quarantine, being only a religion among itinerant and settler
Dyula (Wangara) Muslims whose business was mainly the kola trade. The Dagombas had been
constantly defeated in battle by their neighbours, the Gonjas. At that time the Gonja king, Ndewura
Jakpa had as his chaplain, Muhammad al-Abyad, a Muslim cleric whose mystic powers is reported to
have aided Ndewura Jakpa in the victories that he chalked in battle.10

Believing that the God of the Muslims was a battle winning God, Na Zangina crossed the Sabali river to
see the Muslim cleric there called Sabali Yarna. He is said to have converted to Islam at the hands of the
Sabali Yarna. Acccording to the drum histories, Na Zangina prayed together with the Sabali Yarna facing
east, west, north and south. It is said that there were no travellers to Dagbon, but after these prayers
were said, the roads opened and people travelled by them and Dagbon became prosperous.11
With the conversion of the king of Dagbon to Islam, Islam became the state religion with the Muslim
cleric known in Dagbon as Afa, playing from then on, a pivotal role in the affairs of the Dagomba state.
One can only appreciate how the conversion of Na Zangina could lead to the eventual conversion of
nearly an entire ethnic group, if one understands the reverence that the Dagomba accord their king.
Katz acknowledges that part of the process of acculturation is individual change.12 Especially so, if that

10 Nehemia Levtzion, Muslims & Chiefs in West Africa (London: Oxford University Press, 1968) 120
11 Ivor Wilks, “A Note on the Early Spread of Islam in Dagomba” in Transactions of the Historical Society of Ghana,
Vol 2, 1965, 90
12 D. Katz, “Factors Affecting Social Change: A Socio-Psychological Interpretation” in Journal of Social Issues, 30
(3), 159-180 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
12
individual is as powerful as the Ya-Na. Today there are virtually no indigenous Dagomba customs any
more as all Dagomba customs bear heavy Islamic influences.
Islam and the Security of the Dagomba
 The relationship that developed between Na Zangina and the Afa way back in the eighteenth century
has become a permanent relationship between the paramouncy of Dagbon and Afanema (plural of Afa).
Till date, there is a permanent court Afa, whose duty it is to pray for the safety of the paramount chief
of Dagbon and also, the entire Dagbon State. The Afa in the court of the Ya Na today, is believed to be a
descendant of the Afa who converted Na Zangina to Islam and restored peace and security to the
Dagbon Sate.13
The importance of the Afa in the court of the Ya Na lies in the fact that, he sits in state with the Ya Na,
together with other sub-chiefs. On Mondays and Fridays, he comes out to pray for the security of the
chief and the entire Dagbon Sate. Dagombas believe that these biweekly prayers offered by the Afa are
largely responsible for the peace and security that Dagbon had enjoyed in the past. However, if in spite
of the Afa’s prayers, war breaks out in Dagbon, preparation for war is known as tobu lobbu. Literally
translated, it means the act of “tying up the war” What it means, however, is that, the Afa makes the
attainment of victory a certainty for the Dagomba. This is not just the duty of the court Afa, but all
Afanema residing in the state of Dagbon. During the Konkomba-Dagomba war of 1994 for example,
groups of Afanema competent in the art of tobu lobbu were marshaled, not only to ensure the defeat of
the Konkomba spiritually, but also to actually do physical battle with the Konkomba. The Konkomba are

13 Wilks, “A Note on the Early Spread of Islam…” 90
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
13
the aboriginal people of Eastern Dagbon, who were displaced by the invading band of Na Gbewa’s
descendants. They have remained largely faithful to their traditional religion, even though today a good
number of them profess the Christian faith.14
Imam Husain Rashid was at the head of the group of Afanema from Tamale who did battle with the
Konkomba during the 1994 Dagomba-Konkomba war. He boasted to this researcher that, out of the
contingent of thirty people he led to the battle, only one died. Even so, he died of natural causes.15
Indeed the influence of the Afa is so great on the Dagomba that Dagombas have come to believe that an
attack on them is an attack on Islam. In the case of their war with the Konkomba, the Konkomba did not
help matters by burning down mosques and other religious paraphernalia. This was all the evidence the
Afanema needed to justify their involvement in the war. They did not only get involved but also
declared the war with the Konkomba a jihad. Justifying how an ethnic conflict can be termed jihad,
Imam Hussain Rashid made reference to the Qur’an.
Had Allah not checked one set of people by means of another, there would
surely have been pulled down monasteries, churches, synagogues and
mosques in which the name of Allah is commemorated in abundant measure.
Allah will certainly aid those who aid his cause; for verily Allah is full of
strength, exalted in might.16

14 See Cliff S. Maasole, The Konkomba & their Neighbours: From the Pre-European Period to 1914 (Accra: Ghana
Universities Press, 2006)
15 Imam Hussain Rashid, aged 68, interview by author, tape recording, December 27, 1998.
16 Surat Al-Hajj, 22: 40
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
14
As the one responsible for the security of the Dagbon state, the Afa engages in what is called in Dagbani
as sabli largibu, literally translated as “untying talisman”. At the beginning of each year, Afanema meet
in the court of the Ya Naa to disclose through the Ya Na to the rest of Dagbon, what fate awaits the
people in the coming year. If the coming year portends evil, the Afanema make prescriptions of certain
sacrifices and sadaqat,(voluntary alms giving) which the Ya Na must carry out. This is meant to petition
divine help in averting the impending evil.
The Afanema do this by determining, which Prophet’s spirit will reign within that year. The reigning
Prophet for the year is determined by the day on which the 10th of Muharram falls. Muharram is the
first month of the Muslim calendar.
This is found in a manual, which the Afanema keep and refer to from year to year. For example, if the
10th of Muharram falls on a Sunday, then the reigning Prophet is Adam. In the year of Adam, there will
be plenty of food. Thus it is a year when there will be no hunger. However, a lot of prominent people,
both Muslim and non-Muslim will die within that year.
When the 10th of Muharram falls on Monday, then it is the year of Prophet Iddris (Enoch). This year is a
year of all round prosperity. The other side of it, however, is that, “big” people will use “small” and
weak people as canon fodder. In other words, there will be little sympathy for the weak and vulnerable.
Afanema could not tell how the days of the week came to be assigned to the various prophets, except to
say that this has been handed down to them from generations.
At the individual level too, nearly every Dagomba has a personal Afa who looks after his or her security
and safetly. Some even have more than one Afa. Some have an Afa each to take care of all aspects of 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
15
their endeavors in life: finance, family and health. Thus, most of the time, the Dagomba does not need
to consult the Afa on a problem he faces. The Afa periodically “peeps” into his client’s future and ask
the clients to perform such and such a sacrifice. The Afa wields such great influence on the Dagomba
that a Dagomba man is prepared to forgo an important business transaction if his Afa asks him to do so.
Indeed, the Afa in Dagbon is revered as a teacher of religious lore, as a man of understanding, piety and
power. This Afa first encountered traditional Dagomba society and its king with new methods of
worship and new techniques of divinatory and manipulative control, mostly at a point when traditional
faith was in crisis. At a point when long held beliefs, customs and practices and more importantly,
modes of dominating willful forces of nature seemed inadequate, the Afa offered new insights on
religious change. In Mali, traditionalist sacrifices could not end a drought; Muslim prayer for rain did.17

In Dagbon, the tendana (priest of the Earth-Spirit) could not open up the roads and make Dagbon
prosperous; the Afa did with the aid of the Qur’an.18
The enigmatic Muslim – Arabic worship of the only God practiced by the Afa appeared to the Dagomba
as a more powerful substitute for the sacrifices offered to the tendana (priest of the Earth-Spirit) Equally
enigmatic, Arabic literacy introduced into this previously non-literate population by the Afa, amulets,
techniques of divination and ability of petitionary prayer has been more responsible for attracting the
Dagomba to Islam than other Muslim ideals like those of marriage, statecraft or even quite ironically,

17 Kitab al-Masalik wa’l Mamalik as excerpted in Description de’l Afrique Septentrionale, (trans) and (ed) Mac
Guckin de Slane, 2nd edition (Paris: Genthner, 1913) 175
18 Wilks, “A Note on the Early Spread… 90 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
16
the denial of the existence of other gods. The Dagomba have been used to the use of palpable objects
like charms and amulets before the advent of Islam.
Besides, Islam from the time of the Prophet, had been used to a situation where the traditional practices
of a people among whom it is propagated is allowed to co-exist with the Islamic ones for a while before
they are abolished. Or in some cases, Islam reforms these practices and adopts them for Islam. For
example, hajj. The pilgrimage to the kaba in Makkah, existed as an annual fair in pre- Islamic Arabia
called the ukaz fair.19 The basic difference between the ukaz fair and the hajj today, is that whereas
during the ukaz fair pilgrims worshipped about three hundred and sixty gods housed in the kaba, today,
the hajj is devoted entirely to the worship of the one and only God – Allah.
The Afa had indeed proven over the years to be a perfect substitute for the tendana. From the state as
a whole, headed by the Ya Na to the ordinary Dagomba man or woman, all have placed their destiny
entirely in the hands of the Afa.
Islam and Festivals in Dagbon
So pervasive is the influence of Islam over the Dagomba that nearly all the major festivals of the
Dagomba people bear Islamic identities. Thus what is considered an entirely traditional festival of the
Dagomba people is presided over by not only by the chiefs of Dagbon, but also by the Afanema of
Dagbon.
 The Damba Festival

19 Muhammad Haykal, The Life of Muhammad (Baltimore: North American Trust Publications, 1976) 42 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
17
The Damba festival is the most prominent of all Dagomba festivals. It is also the name of the month in
which the festival is celebrated. It is supposed to be a festival of thanks giving to God for a prosperous
year gone by. However, the month in which the Damba festival is celebrated, coincides with Rabi-ulAwwal on the Muslim Calender. Rabi-ul-Awwal is the third month of the Muslim calendar. Incidentally,
Rabi-ul-Awwal is the month in which the Prophet was born. Thus, Damba today is celebrated not just as
a festival of thanks giving but also a festival commemorating the birth of the Prophet Muhammad.
Damba is celebrated on two different days in the month of Rabi-ul-Awwal. The 10th and the 17th of the
month. The celebration on the 10th is known as Somo Damba (Damba for the masses) and the
celebration of the 17th as Na Damba (Chief’s Damba).20 Even though the Prophet was born on the 12th of
Rabi-ul-Awwal, Dagombas say that 10th day celebration is meant to coincide with the Prophet’s birth and
the 17th to coincide with the day of his naming.
In the celebration itself, two major events are observed; the ceremony of rice picking and the dancing
around the Damba cow. At the ceremony of rice picking, rice is poured on a piece of cloth. Afanema
then sit in a circle around the rice. As they pick the rice grain by grain, they sing praises to the Prophet
Mohammad asking God’s blessing on him. They also ask the Prophet to intercede with God on behalf of
the people of Dagbon.21 The significance of this rice picking is that, the rice actually represents all
grains. Thus, it is a way of thanking God for the year’s harvest of grains: rice, maize and millet. As this is
being done, the chiefs also form another circle around the Afanema and look on as the Afanema do the

20 Mohammed Yussif Alhassan, “Islam & Tradition: A Case Study of the Damba Festival of the Dagomba People of
Northern Ghana” A Dissertation Presented to the Department of Religious Studies of the University of Cape Coast
in Partial Fulfilment of the Requirements for the Award of a Bachelor of Arts (Honours) Degree, 1998. 25
21 Alhassan, “Islam & Tradition:…” 25 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
18
picking. Both the Afanema and the traditional chiefs of the Dagomba people do the dancing around the
Damba cow. As they go round the cow, the Afanema sing the praises of the Prophet. What was an
entirely traditional festival of the Dagomba people, has become almost a Muslim festival presided over
by Afanema.
 The Bugum or Fire Festival
The Bugum Festival is another testimony of how influential the Islam has become in Dagomba life and
thought. The fire festival is celebrated in Dagbon once a year, two months after the Muslim Festival of
Id-ul-Adha. This festival is celebrated in the night. Young and old, man and woman, boy and girl all
make preparations for the festival by gathering dry stalks. These stalks are put together and tied by a
rope or twine. When night falls, all gather in front of the house of the local chief with their bound stalks.
The chief then comes out to address them, wishing them a happy celebration. The stalks are then lit and
the people, in a long procession, begin their march through the town to the outskirts of the town. At
the outskirts, the stalks with the fire still burning are thrown onto a tree designated for the purpose.
After the fire is disposed of in this manner, the celebrants cut shrubs and leaves from whatever tree is in
sight and begin their march back to the chief’s palace. This is done amid singing and dancing. These are
mostly war songs. In front of chief’s palace, they continue to dance around in a circle. As the singing
and dancing are going on, the boys, girls, women and men of the palace sprinkle a concoction on them.
This concoction is made up of written verses and chapters of the Qur’an. This had been prepared earlier
in the day by a group of Muslim clerics. Everybody struggles to make sure that the concoction touches
him or her. They also make sure their shrubs and leaves are soaked with the concoction. The people 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
19
believe that it contains baraka (blessing) and has a healing effect. At home people boil these leaves and
shrubs and add it to their bathing water. The conflicting myths about the origin of the Bugum Festival
are in itself, a testimony of how influential Islam has become in Dagomba life and thought. One myth
has it that, the festival is celebrated in commemoration of the day the ark of Noah (Prophet Nuhu in
Islam) hit dry land after the floods had subsided. It is related that after the ark had hit dry land, the
place was so dark that the people had to light touches to find their way out of the ark.
Another myth has it that it is in commemoration of the day when a search party found a Dagomba King’s
son who had been lost for several days. It is said that the search for the King’s son took place at night
and in the daytime. At night, the search party had to light touches in order to conduct the search. It
was in the course of the search one night that the search party found the boy sleeping under a tree at
the outskirts of that town. The search party then threw their torches unto the tree in an attempt to
burn it for harbouring the King’s son, thus causing the king a lot of anxiety. Ever since, the event had
been commemorated in the form of the Bugum festival.
Thus, Islam has become the defining factor in the celebration of Dagomba Festivals. It is therefore
almost impossible to distinguish an indigenous Dagomba festival.
Islam and Rites Of Passage in Dagbon
Naming, marriage and death ceremonies in Dagbon all show considerable Islamic influences. So
considerable is the Islamic influence on these rites that none of them are performed without the 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
20
involvement of Muslim clerics. Rites of passage in Islam confirm to a large extent, Dupuis’ claim that in
Dagbon, the Islamic civil code serves both the infidel and the believers.22
 Naming Rites
In Dagbon, a woman is assisted to give birth by a woman traditional birth attendant. Men are not
allowed into the place where a woman is to give birth. On the eighth day after the woman has given
birth, the naming ceremony is held. However, prior to the eighth day, the father of the child would have
chosen or short-listed three names, from which a Muslim cleric is supposed to confer one on the child.
These names are given to the Muslim cleric three days prior to the naming ceremony. It is the Muslim
cleric’s duty to choose a name among the list for the child.
Even though the father of the child would have his most preferred name among the list, the Muslim
cleric is not compelled to adhere to the will of the father. The father does not even tell the Muslim
cleric which of the names he prefers. The reason why these names are given to the Muslim cleric ahead
of the naming ceremony is to enable the Muslim cleric consult God on which of the names is suitable for
the child. In actual fact, what the Muslim cleric does is to refer to a manual containing over a thousand
names. These names are the names of mostly, companions of the Prophet and people who played a
role in the early history of Islam. They therefore check from the manual to find out which companion of
the Prophet or prominent Muslim personality was born on the same day and time as the child. The child
is then given the same name as the companion. Indeed any Arab name passes for a Muslim name. These
days however, the influence of education and modernity has resulted in Dagombas adoting their own

22 Joseph Dupuis, Journal of a Residence in Ashantee (2nd Edition) (London: Frank Cass, 1966) ciii-civ 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
21
indigenous names for their children. There is now an awareness that one can be fully Muslim even with
an indigenous Dagomba name. . But even parents, who insist on giving their children traditional names,
add it to the Islamic name given by the Muslim cleric. Thus it is not uncommon to find Dagombas called
Muhammad Dawuni with Dawunni being their traditional name.
Most of the time therefore, the Muslim cleric’s choice does not even conform to the list presented by
the child’s father. In that case, the Muslim cleric proposes an entirely different name. Sometimes
names of children in Dagbon are not the exact names that these companions of the Prophet bore, but
praise-names or adjectives by which they were also known. For example, this author was bon on
Monday. The prophet Muhammad was also born on Monday. However, the Prophet was also called
Mustapha, so this author was named Mustapha. By and large, there is always an Islamic basis for the
name of a Dagomba, Muslim or non-Muslim. On the day of the naming ceremony, the presiding cleric
invites other clerics to help him in the ceremony. These clerics come to the ceremony to pray for the
new born baby and to commit the child into the hands of God. After this a sheep is slaughtered as a
sacrifice to God. After the sacrifice, the name of the child is written on a wall in the house, so that all
that come into the house know the name of the child. 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
22
 Marriage Rites
Marriage is compulsory for every adult in the traditional Dagomba milieu. Thus, once one has attained
the age of maturity, one cannot refuse to marry. Generally, beyond a certain age, an unmarried person
if he is a man is branded irresponsible and a prostitute if a woman. The same applies to the case of
Islam. The Prophet is reported to have said; “whoever is able to marry, should marry, for that will help
him lower his gaze and guard his modesty.”23 The age at which a male is considered mature in Dagbon is
twenty and for a female, sixteen. However, due to the influence of the Islam, one can find men and
women younger than these stipulated ages who are married. This is because in Islam, boys as young as
sixteen or seventeen and females as young as twelve or thirteen could marry. The Prophet is reported
to have married Aisha when she was about twelve-years old.
Dagombas were already polygamous people. Hence they did not find it difficult accepting the
polygamous doctrines of Islam. Even though the traditional Dagomba practice is to marry as many
women as one is capable of, Islam imposes a restriction of four wives. Thus, even Dagombas who are
not Muslims do not exceed the limits set by Islam except for chiefs who sometimes have up to forty
wives.
Traditional Dagomba marriages are presided over by Muslim clerics. This is called Amilya lobbu, literally
“tying of marriage’. Even though in traditional Dagomba practice, the bride price is what serves to seal a
marriage between couples, the Muslim cleric must “tie the marriage” before it is considered sealed.
Thus even non-Muslim Dagombas will have to invite a Muslim cleric to “tie their marriage” for them, if

23 Bukhari, Book 31, Hadith 129 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
23
that marriage is to be recognized by the rest of society. At the “tying ceremony”, the Muslim cleric
expounds on the Islamic teachings on marriage and sets the standard by which the couple should live as
husband and wife.
 Funeral Rites
The Muslim cleric presides over the death ceremonies of the Dagomba. When a Dagomba dies, the
head of the family sends someone to inform the Muslim cleric and subsequently all other extended
family members of the deceased. Preparation for burial begins immediately. The head of the family
sends someone to buy white calico, to be used in wrapping the dead for burial.
Before the introduction of Islam into Dagbon, they first used leaves. These leaves were called
Kornamgbandi. They later changed to the use of the material used for sewing smock. Barely three hours
after the death of a person, preparation for burial begins. It is hoped that by this time quite a number of
the deceased’s family members would have arrived at the funeral ground. The body is then washed.
Males wash males and females wash females. Females are not allowed to go to the graveyard. It is
believed that women are weak hearted and may weep at the graveyard. Weeping over the death of a
relative is discouraged in Islam.
Rather the bereaved is encouraged on hearing of the death of a relative, to say: “inna lilahi wa inna ilaihi
raji’un” which means, from Allah we came, to him we shall return. (Q:2:157). After the dead body is
bathed and shroud in the calico, it is placed in a coffin and placed in an open space. Relatives and
sympathizers then line up behind the coffin to pray for the dead. This is a special prayer called SalatulJanaza. A Muslim cleric leads this prayer session. Even though Muslims are not supposed to pray for 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
24
non-Muslims this rule is hardly adhered to in Dagbon. Once the fellow had ever said the Muslim ritual
prayers in the presence of someone who can confirm it, the Janaza prayers is said for the person. In
Dagbon therefore, it is customary to say, “he was praying” rather than “he was a Muslim”.
After this the coffin is put into a vehicle and driven to the cemetery for burial. At the graveyard the
body is removed from the coffin and lowered into the grave with the face towards the Kaba. After the
grave is filled with sand, the Muslim cleric then preaches a short sermon on the need for virtuous living.
He also prays for the souls of the departed. After this the people go back to the house of the dead for a
short gathering. They then disperse to await the third, seventh and fortieth day ceremonies. Thus three
days after the death of a Dagomb, then on the seventh and again on the fortieth day, relatives and well
wishers gather to pray for the departed. A Muslim cleric also leads this prayer. The fortieth day
therefore brings to an end the funeral ceremonies.
Rites of passage in Dagbon are therefore presided over by a Muslim cleric. Indeed, the Muslim cleric is
central to all the rites of passage. This is the case for both Muslim, and non-Muslim.
Conclusion
Indeed Islam in Dagbon is a classical study of the interaction between two very different cultures.
Meeting in the eighteenth century, these hitherto completely different cultures have virtually crystalised
and woven themselves into a beautiful symmetry that makes it difficult for the close observer to
distinguish the original elements of these cultures.
Cultures migrate and in today’s technological world, cultures cannot develop in isolation. The Dagomba
and Islamic cultures provide a classical case for anthropologists and philosophers on how one culture 
The Int. Conference on Universalism, Relatavism & Intercultural
Philosophy. Univ of Cape Coast, 3-5 Feb, 2010
25
can be transformed by interaction with another. Acculturation studies identify one culture, especially
the “newcomer” culture as dominant.24 In this case, Islam, which is the “newcomer” culture has
dominated the indigenous Dagomba culture. When cultures migrate and integrate with host cultures, do
the synthesis become a distinct culture, or do they become a new form of the culture? Can Dagomba
culture today be truly said to be purely Dagomba or is it an Islamo-Dagomba culture?
Whatever our postulations are, Dagombas themselves are proud of this blend of their indigenous
cultures and the Islamic tradition. In the words of an elder Dagomba, “these traditions have been
bequethed to us from generations.”25 Indeed from generation to generation, this is what today can be
termed Dagomba culture.
